SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states its modeling and decoding formalism (Equations 1, 2, 17, 18) and supports claims with empirical results (e.g. single and ensemble F1 reported on CoNLL-2005/2012). The constrained A* decoding heuristic is well-motivated and claimed to be admissible, and they use the official CoNLL evaluation script which supports validity of reported numbers. Minor issues are absence of statistical significance testing and the decision to not enforce R constraints (noted because gold data often violates them) could use additional justification, but these do not undermine the main conclusions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper combines several existing techniques (deep BiLSTMs, highway connections, RNN-dropout, constrained decoding, product-of-experts ensembling) and applies them to SRL to push state-of-the-art. The formulation as BIO tagging and use of A* constrained decoding are adaptations of prior ideas (Zhou and Xu 2015; Lewis and Steedman 2014) rather than wholly new paradigms. The contribution is a non-trivial engineering and empirical advance with careful analysis, but conceptually incremental over recent LSTM-based SRL work.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized into clear sections (model, constrained decoding, experiments, analysis) and lists its concrete differences from prior work early (simplified I/O, highway connections, recurrent dropout, BIO decoding, PoE ensemble). Notation for the scoring function and decoding heuristic is explicit (with numbered equations) and figures/ablation plots are used to illustrate results. Some repeated/garbled lines appear in the provided text fragment, but the overall exposition of method and experiments is readable and logically presented.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Experiments are thorough: evaluation on two standard PropBank datasets (CoNLL-2005 and CoNLL-2012), ablations (highway connections, RNN-dropout, orthonormal initialization), ensemble setup, and detailed analyses (error types, long-range dependencies, syntax impact). They report use of official evaluation scripts, compare to multiple baselines, and examine failure modes (adjunct/PP attachment, SRL constraint violations), providing sufficient detail and diagnostic experiments to support their claims and reproducibility.

IMPACT.justification: The work addresses an important, long-studied NLP task (SRL) and demonstrates strong empirical gains (e.g. 83.2 F1 on CoNLL-2005 ensemble) while questioning the necessity of syntactic input, which is likely to influence subsequent research directions. By showing deep end-to-end models can recover long-range dependencies and still benefit from syntax, the paper meaningfully shifts practitioner and researcher perspectives on model design for SRL.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic, methods (deep BiLSTMs, constrained decoding), datasets (CoNLL shared tasks), and evaluation standards are squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML conferences. The paper addresses the right audience and uses evaluation practices expected at such venues (official scripts, comparison to recent baselines).

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate recent baselines (Zhou and Xu 2015; FitzGerald et al. 2015; Täckström et al. 2015 and others), uses the same datasets and official evaluation script, and analyzes differences (e.g. effect of syntax from different parsers and gold syntax). It also provides ablations and references to prior systems whose predictions were available for comparison, yielding comprehensive and fair comparisons.
